text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY - BMT SURVIVORSHIP CLINIC
OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE

PATIENT: Marcus T. Delgado
DOB: on file
ATTENDING: [Attending Hematologist/Oncologist]
FELLOW: [BMT Fellow]
DIAGNOSIS: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), NOS, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A R882H, ELN 2022 adverse risk; status post 7+3 induction with midostaurin, CR1 MRD-negative; status post matched unrelated donor allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (MUD allo-HSCT), myeloablative FluBu4 conditioning
TRANSPLANT DATE: [day 0 on file]
VISIT TYPE: Scheduled Annual Follow-Up / Survivorship Transition Visit (approximately 12-13 months post-transplant)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Delgado is a 49-year-old male with a history of de novo AML (FLT3-ITD, ELN adverse risk) presenting for his 12-month post-transplant annual surveillance visit. He reports feeling well overall and has resumed his normal daily activities. He has no acute complaints today.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Delgado was diagnosed with de novo AML approximately 13 months ago after presenting with a three-week history of progressive fatigue, pallor, gingival hypertrophy with spontaneous mucosal bleeding, and epistaxis. At presentation, his WBC was markedly elevated at 84,000/uL with 62% circulating blasts, hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL, and platelets 28,000/uL. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed AML with 78% blasts, normal diploid karyotype (46,XY), and next-generation sequencing demonstrated a high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD (VAF 47%, AR 0.87) with a co-occurring DNMT3A R882H mutation (VAF 42%), placing him in the ELN 2022 adverse-risk category. CSF cytology was negative. He had no evidence of extramedullary disease on staging CT, though he had mild hepatosplenomegaly consistent with leukemic infiltration.

He underwent standard 7+3 induction chemotherapy with cytarabine continuous infusion plus daunorubicin, and midostaurin 50 mg PO twice daily on days 8-21 per the RATIFY regimen. Induction was complicated by febrile neutropenia with an ANC nadir of zero (cultures negative), grade 2 oral mucositis, and grade 2 nausea/vomiting requiring a brief midostaurin hold. He achieved complete morphologic remission after a single induction cycle, with MRD negativity confirmed by 10-color multiparameter flow cytometry (sensitivity 10^-4) and FLT3-ITD PCR negativity.

Given his adverse-risk disease, he proceeded expeditiously to allogeneic transplant in CR1. A 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor (CMV seropositive) was identified through the NMDP. He received myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine 30 mg/m2 x 5 days plus busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day x 4 days (FluBu4) with busulfan PK monitoring and seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam. GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus plus short-course Trexall (days +1, +3, +6, +11). G-CSF-mobilized peripheral blood stem cells were infused on day 0 (CD34+ dose 5.8 x 10^6/kg). The immediate post-transplant course was notable for neutrophil engraftment on day +14, platelet engraftment on day +19, grade 2 oral mucositis requiring IV morphine PCA and supplemental tube feeds for approximately 8 days, and culture-negative febrile neutropenia with prompt response to empiric cefepime. He had no SOS/VOD, no significant hepatotoxicity, and only grade 1 tacrolimus-related nephrotoxicity and tremors, both managed with dose adjustments.

Day +30 bone marrow biopsy demonstrated trilineage engraftment with near-complete donor chimerism (whole blood 97%, myeloid 99%, T-cell 91%), and no morphologic evidence of residual AML. Subsequent evaluations at day +45, day +100, and day +180 all confirmed ongoing complete remission with deepening donor chimerism. By day +180, whole blood chimerism was 99% donor, T-cell chimerism 96%, myeloid chimerism 100% donor. MRD by flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD PCR remained negative through day +180. DNMT3A R882H digital droplet PCR was also negative at day +180.

During months 2-4 post-transplant, he developed mild chronic GVHD (NIH consensus criteria: mild) affecting skin (palmar erythema and scaling, less than 18% BSA, no sclerosis) and oral mucosa (lichenoid changes of buccal mucosa, grade 1). There was no GI, hepatic, pulmonary, or ocular GVHD. He required no systemic corticosteroids; his skin GVHD was managed with topical triamcinolone and his oral GVHD with topical budesonide rinse and magic mouthwash. Tacrolimus was successfully tapered and discontinued at month 8 post-transplant without any flare of GVHD. His skin GVHD fully resolved. Oral GVHD showed significant improvement, with only faint asymptomatic lichenoid buccal changes at his 9-month visit.

At the 12-month post-transplant surveillance CT (performed approximately 2 weeks prior to today's visit), no lymphadenopathy, no pulmonary abnormalities, no hepatosplenomegaly, and no osseous lesions concerning for extramedullary disease were identified. Today's visit represents his formal one-year post-transplant annual review and marks the beginning of the transition to long-term survivorship follow-up.

Since his last visit (approximately 3 months ago), Mr. Delgado reports a full return to his pre-illness functional level. He is back to full-time teaching of high school science and has resumed recreational sports, including weekend tennis. He reports no new constitutional symptoms. He denies fever, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. He denies any new skin changes, rashes, or pruritus. No new oral symptoms, dysphagia, or xerostomia. No GI complaints (no diarrhea, no abdominal cramping, normal bowel habits). No respiratory symptoms. No musculoskeletal pain. No visual changes or dry eyes. No hematuria or dysuria.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: No fever, no chills, no night sweats, no weight loss. Energy is excellent; patient describes himself as "back to normal."
Skin: No new rashes, no pruritus. Mild residual skin dryness noted on bilateral hands, improved from prior visits.
HEENT: No oral pain, no difficulty chewing or swallowing. No xerostomia. No epistaxis. No visual symptoms, no dry eyes.
Cardiopulmonary: No dyspnea, no chest pain, no palpitations. No cough.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea, no vomiting, no diarrhea, no abdominal pain. Appetite excellent.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, no hematuria. Menses not applicable (male patient).
Musculoskeletal: No joint swelling, no arthralgias, no myalgias. No muscle cramping.
Neurologic: No headaches, no focal weakness or sensory changes, no cognitive complaints. No tremors (had mild tacrolimus-related tremors previously, now resolved after discontinuation).
Endocrine: Reports mild fatigue with physical exertion at high temperature, possibly heat sensitivity; otherwise no symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism. Libido mildly reduced but improved from earlier in recovery; acknowledges this may reflect post-transplant gonadal effects.
Psychosocial: Good adjustment. No depression or anxiety by patient report. Declines formal psychosocial support referral again today; states family support is strong.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital signs: Temperature 36.8 C, Blood pressure 132/80 mmHg, Heart rate 74 bpm, Respiratory rate 14, O2 saturation 99% on room air, Weight 82 kg (stable, baseline), BMI 26.3.
Performance status: ECOG 0.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished male in no distress. Alert, pleasant, engaged in discussion. Appears healthy.
Skin: No petechiae, no ecchymoses. No macular or papular rashes. Bilateral palms: trace residual skin dryness/scaling without erythema (improved from prior grade 1 palmar GVHD). No sclerodermatous changes. No poikiloderma. No livedo.
Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Anicteric sclerae. Conjunctivae pink. No injection or discharge. Oropharynx: bilateral buccal mucosa with very faint residual lichenoid changes (nearly fully resolved compared to prior visit); no erythema, no ulceration, no pseudomembrane. No trismus. No xerostomia apparent on exam. Lips and tongue normal.
Neck: Supple. No cervical, submandibular, or posterior cervical lymphadenopathy.
Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi. Good air entry.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Liver edge not palpable below costal margin. Spleen not palpable. No masses. Normal bowel sounds.
Extremities: No pitting edema. No erythema or swelling of joints. Good peripheral pulses.
Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. No tremor. Coordination intact. Gait normal.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC (today):
  - WBC: 7.4 k/uL
  - ANC: 5,100/uL
  - Hemoglobin: 14.0 g/dL
  - Hematocrit: 41.8%
  - MCV: 90 fL
  - Platelets: 231,000/uL
  All values within normal limits. Full hematopoietic recovery confirmed.

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 140 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (normalized from prior mild tacrolimus-related elevation), BUN: 14 mg/dL
  - AST: 28 U/L, ALT: 31 U/L, Total Bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL, Alkaline Phosphatase: 72 U/L (all within normal limits; hepatic GVHD resolved)
  - Albumin: 4.3 g/dL, Total protein 7.4 g/dL

TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (within normal limits; thyroid function screen as part of long-term late effects monitoring)
Morning serum testosterone: 312 ng/dL (low-normal; scheduled endocrine referral for further evaluation, consistent with possible post-transplant gonadal suppression)
LH: 12 IU/L, FSH: 18 IU/L (mildly elevated, consistent with subclinical gonadal dysfunction)

Molecular / Infectious Surveillance:
  - FLT3-ITD PCR (peripheral blood): NEGATIVE (sensitivity 10^-4)
  - CMV PCR (peripheral blood): undetectable
  - EBV PCR (peripheral blood): undetectable
  - BK virus urine PCR: not sent (no urinary symptoms)

Immunoglobulins (sent for late immune reconstitution assessment):
  - IgG: 684 mg/dL (mildly below lower limit of normal of 700; monitor; does not yet meet threshold for IVIG replacement)
  - IgA: 98 mg/dL (low-normal)
  - IgM: 52 mg/dL (low-normal, expected at this time point)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (performed approximately 2 weeks prior, reviewed today):
No mediastinal, hilar, axillary, or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Lungs are clear without pulmonary nodules, consolidation, pleural effusion, or air trapping. Cardiac silhouette is normal. Liver is normal in size with homogeneous enhancement and no focal lesions. Spleen measures 8.8 cm, normal in size and homogeneous. No adrenal, renal, or pelvic abnormalities. Osseous structures show no focal lytic or blastic lesions; marrow density appears homogeneous throughout. Overall appearance is entirely unremarkable, with complete resolution of the prior hepatosplenomegaly that was present at diagnosis and no sign of leukemic recurrence or chloroma anywhere in the imaged field.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A R882H, ELN 2022 adverse risk) — COMPLETE REMISSION, approximately 12-13 months post-MUD allo-HSCT.

Mr. Delgado continues to do exceptionally well. All CBC parameters are fully normalized and stable. FLT3-ITD PCR from peripheral blood is negative. CMV and EBV viremia surveillance are both undetectable. The surveillance CT from 2 weeks ago is completely clean with no concerning findings. He has achieved a sustained, deep complete remission now confirmed by serial PCR monitoring, full donor chimerism (last confirmed at day +180), and normal imaging. He is asymptomatic and fully functional at ECOG 0.

This is a meaningful milestone given his adverse-risk biology — FLT3-ITD with an allelic ratio of 0.87 and co-mutation of DNMT3A R882H, both of which are associated with a higher relapse risk in the transplant setting. The depth and durability of his current response are encouraging. His cumulative post-transplant trajectory — MRD-negative CR1 prior to transplant, rapid engraftment, full donor chimerism by day +30, absence of any relapse signal at 12 months — is reassuring. While relapses can still occur beyond 12 months in this patient population, and long-term vigilance remains essential, his overall prognosis is now meaningfully more favorable than at the time of initial risk stratification. Estimated disease-free survival in the setting of MRD-negative CR1 transplant with full donor chimerism for ELN adverse-risk AML is approximately 50-60% at 2 years, and his trajectory places him among the more favorable subset of this group.

Plan:
  - Continue quarterly CBC and FLT3-ITD PCR peripheral blood monitoring.
  - Next formal bone marrow biopsy with chimerism and MRD assessment planned at 2 years post-transplant per institutional protocol, unless clinical or laboratory signals prompt earlier evaluation.
  - Annual surveillance CT for at least one additional year (next at approximately 24 months post-transplant). Will re-evaluate need for ongoing cross-sectional imaging thereafter based on clinical course.
  - No disease-directed therapy indicated.

2. Chronic GVHD — RESOLVED (skin and oral mucosa).

Mild chronic GVHD affecting skin and oral mucosa has essentially resolved. Palmar skin GVHD is fully resolved. Oral GVHD is near-complete resolution with only trace asymptomatic lichenoid buccal changes remaining. No active GVHD in any organ system. Patient is off all immunosuppression (tacrolimus discontinued at month 8 without flare). No systemic corticosteroids were ever required. This represents an excellent GVHD course.

Plan:
  - Topical corticosteroids and topical budesonide rinse may be formally discontinued given near-complete resolution of oral GVHD and absence of symptoms. Instructed patient to resume as needed if any oral symptoms recur.
  - Continue sun protection counseling given prior skin GVHD and ongoing immunosuppression-related skin risk (though now off immunosuppression, cumulative risk counseling appropriate).
  - Dermatology referral offered for routine skin cancer surveillance; patient accepts referral.
  - Continue monitoring liver function tests and pulmonary function at annual visits for late GVHD reactivation, though no current concern.

3. Immune reconstitution — ONGOING, low-normal immunoglobulins.

As expected at 12-13 months post-transplant, humoral immune reconstitution is still in progress. IgG is mildly below the normal lower limit at 684 mg/dL. IgA and IgM are low-normal. This is a common and expected finding at this time point following MUD allo-HSCT, particularly in the setting of prior calcineurin inhibitor-based GVHD prophylaxis. He has had no significant infections.

Plan:
  - Recheck immunoglobulins in 3 months. No IVIG replacement indicated at this time (IgG above 400 mg/dL threshold used in our practice for asymptomatic patients).
  - Continue acyclovir antiviral prophylaxis (through month 18-24, per institutional protocol for seropositive patients).
  - Continue PCP prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (to continue until off immunosuppression for 6 months and CD4 count recovers — reassess at next visit).
  - Antifungal prophylaxis: Fluconazole discontinued at this visit given adequate immune recovery and absence of GVHD requiring immunosuppression.
  - CD4 count sent today; result pending. Will review at next visit to guide duration of PCP prophylaxis.

4. Vaccination — IN PROGRESS.

Vaccination series was initiated at 6 months post-transplant. To date, the patient has received inactivated influenza, pneumococcal conjugate (PCV15/20 per updated recommendations), and Tdap. Live vaccines (MMR, varicella) are deferred until at least month 24 post-transplant in the setting of prior GVHD, and only after confirmation that the patient is fully off all immunosuppression and has adequate immune reconstitution.

Plan:
  - Hepatitis B vaccine series to be initiated today (dose 1 administered in clinic).
  - Meningococcal vaccine recommended; prescription provided.
  - Inactivated influenza (seasonal) to be administered at next visit if not yet received for current season.
  - Reassess eligibility for live vaccines at month 24 visit.

5. Late transplant effects monitoring — INITIATED.

  a. Bone density: DEXA scan ordered at last visit; results reviewed today. Lumbar spine T-score -1.3 (osteopenia). Hip T-score -0.9 (normal). Given prior myeloablative conditioning with busulfan, corticosteroid exposure (low but present from prophylaxis regimens), and post-transplant gonadal suppression, osteopenia is an expected and clinically relevant finding.
     Plan: Calcium 1,200 mg/day and vitamin D 1,000 IU/day supplementation recommended and prescribed. Repeat DEXA in 2 years. Endocrine co-management recommended (see below). Weight-bearing exercise encouraged.

  b. Gonadal function / Endocrine: Morning testosterone 312 ng/dL (low-normal). FSH 18 IU/L and LH 12 IU/L mildly elevated, consistent with subclinical hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, likely secondary to prior myeloablative conditioning. Patient notes mildly reduced libido.
     Plan: Endocrine referral placed today for testosterone replacement therapy evaluation and comprehensive gonadal assessment, including semen analysis if patient desires fertility counseling. Discussed with Mr. Delgado that myeloablative busulfan-based conditioning carries a high risk of permanent azoospermia and hypogonadism; he reports that he did not bank sperm prior to transplant (this was discussed at that time and he elected against it). He appears to have come to terms with this. Endocrinology to determine whether testosterone replacement is appropriate.

  c. Thyroid: TSH 2.1 mIU/L — normal. Annual TSH monitoring to continue per late effects protocol.

  d. Ophthalmology: Referral placed for cataract screening. Busulfan-based conditioning is associated with increased risk of posterior subcapsular cataracts. Patient has no visual complaints at this time.

  e. Cardiovascular: Blood pressure today 132/80 mmHg. Patient has a prior history of well-controlled hypertension managed with lisinopril 10 mg daily (pre-existing, continued throughout). Will continue to monitor BP at each visit. Annual lipid panel ordered (result pending); relevant given long-term cardiovascular risk in transplant survivors. Anthracycline exposure (cumulative daunorubicin dose approximately 350 mg/m2 total — 3 doses of 60 mg/m2 during induction) is within the range requiring periodic cardiac surveillance. Echocardiogram will be ordered at the 2-year post-transplant visit.

  f. Secondary malignancy screening: Patient counseled regarding the modestly elevated long-term risk of secondary malignancies in transplant survivors, including post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD, EBV-driven — low risk given EBV PCR undetectable), secondary myelodysplastic syndrome/AML (uncommon, more relevant in setting of conditioning regimen), and therapy-related solid tumors (very long-term). Annual skin exam recommended. Age-appropriate colorectal cancer screening noted (family history of colon cancer in maternal uncle at 62; patient will be 50 in approximately 2 years — colonoscopy at age 50 or earlier will be discussed).

  g. Metabolic: Weight stable at 82 kg, BMI 26.3. Fasting glucose and HbA1c ordered as part of metabolic surveillance (results pending). Encouraged Mediterranean-style diet and regular physical activity.

6. Psychosocial:

Mr. Delgado continues to report excellent psychosocial adjustment. He has returned to full-time work as a high school science teacher, which he finds deeply meaningful and normalizing. He is physically active and engaged with family and friends. He declined formal psychosocial support referral at this visit, as at prior visits. He was reminded that the support is available at any time should he feel the need. He was also counseled about the role of peer support networks for AML/transplant survivors, such as those offered through the Leukemia & Lymphoma Society, and was provided with resources.

7. Follow-up:

  - Return to BMT survivorship clinic in 3 months for CBC, metabolic panel, FLT3-ITD PCR, CMV/EBV surveillance, immunoglobulin recheck, and review of pending labs (lipid panel, fasting glucose, HbA1c, CD4 count).
  - Transition to hematology/oncology survivorship program will proceed gradually; patient will continue joint follow-up between BMT program and general survivorship clinic.
  - All referrals placed today: endocrinology, ophthalmology, dermatology.
  - Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and had no further questions. He expressed gratitude for the care team's support throughout his treatment course. Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes including examination, counseling, and education.

Electronically signed by: [Attending Hematologist/Oncologist, BMT Program]
Co-signed by: [BMT Fellow]
